KYA KYC / KYB Extension Large US Bank Zero-Trust Agent Identity
Deep Technical Low-Level Design and Implementation Document

Know Your Agent (KYA) as a first-class control in enterprise KYC and KYB

This design defines KYA as the identity, attestation, authorization, behavior governance, and auditability layer for autonomous and semi-autonomous AI agents acting on behalf of a natural person, legal entity, employee, internal line of business, or regulated third party. The design assumes a large US financial institution with layered defense requirements across retail banking, treasury, payments, capital markets, vendor operations, and AML/BSA control environments.

4

Identity dimensions

Agent identity, principal linkage, capability scope, accountable owner.
5

Core planes

Registration, attestation, runtime authZ, monitoring, evidence & audit.
3

Operating modes

Assistive, bounded autonomy, high-trust delegated execution.
0

Standing secrets

Target state is secretless runtime identity with short-lived credentials.

1. Scope and design goals

KYA extends traditional KYC and KYB because banks already validate humans and businesses, but they do not natively validate autonomous software actors that can retrieve data, make decisions, invoke tools, initiate workflows, or request movement of funds. The institution must establish cryptographic identity, delegation provenance, runtime trust, action-level accountability, and revocation for every agent that can affect a regulated workflow.

In scope

  • Customer-facing AI agents acting for retail or commercial customers.
  • Internal employee copilots with action rights across core banking, CRM, payments, and case systems.
  • Vendor and partner agents integrated via APIs or network channels.
  • Sub-agents, planner/worker patterns, retrieval agents, monitoring agents, and settlement agents.
  • Agent-initiated onboarding, document review, sanctions screening, transaction assembly, case creation, and payment orchestration.

Non-goals

  • Replacing enterprise IAM, PAM, or KYC/KYB systems of record.
  • Treating prompt text as identity proof.
  • Allowing unrestricted autonomous action in high-risk AML or funds-movement decisions.
  • Assuming model risk governance alone is sufficient for agent governance.
Design principle: KYA should be inserted as a reusable control plane beside KYC/KYB, not implemented as one-off logic inside each AI product. Every agent action must be attributable to an agent credential, a verified principal, a delegated intent, a policy decision, and tamper-evident audit evidence.

2. Reference architecture

The implementation uses a split-plane architecture: an identity and trust plane, a policy and decision plane, an execution plane, an observability and evidence plane, and a regulatory integration plane. This separation keeps onboarding, issuance, policy, and runtime enforcement independently scalable and auditable.

Infographic architecture

Executive and engineering views

These infographic diagrams summarize the KYA control stack from verified principal to controlled execution, then break the platform into trust, policy, execution, and evidence modules.

High-level story

KYA extends KYC and KYB by introducing agent identity, delegation provenance, runtime trust, and audit evidence as first-class controls.

Low-level control path

Every sensitive action passes through attestation, credential issuance, policy evaluation, approval, execution, and post-action monitoring.

Infographic view

Enterprise trust stack

KYA sits beside KYC and KYB, then feeds runtime controls across payments, onboarding, AML, and servicing.

flowchart TB subgraph TOP[Enterprise Trust Stack] KYC[KYC\nKnow the person] KYB[KYB\nKnow the business] KYA[KYA\nKnow the agent] end subgraph MID[Decision Fabric] P[Policy and Delegation] R[Risk Scoring] H[Human Approval] end subgraph BOT[Execution Domains] C1[Customer Servicing] C2[KYC and KYB Ops] C3[AML and OFAC] C4[Payments and Wires] C5[Core Banking] end KYC --> P KYB --> P KYA --> P P --> R R --> H H --> C1 H --> C2 H --> C3 H --> C4 H --> C5 classDef top fill:#dff4f2,stroke:#01696f,color:#163235,stroke-width:2px; classDef mid fill:#eef2fb,stroke:#5d78b8,color:#20304d,stroke-width:2px; classDef bot fill:#f8f3ea,stroke:#b87a28,color:#4a2f12,stroke-width:2px; class KYC,KYB,KYA top; class P,R,H mid; class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 bot;
Infographic view

KYA control story

A simplified narrative from verified principal to monitored autonomous execution.

flowchart LR A[1. Verified Principal\nCustomer • Business • Employee • Vendor] --> B[2. KYA Registration\nAgent ID • Owner • Risk Tier • Scope] B --> C[3. Trust Establishment\nAttestation • Short-lived Credential • Assurance Level] C --> D[4. Controlled Execution\nPolicy Decision • Tool Access • Human Approval] D --> E[5. Continuous Oversight\nBehavior Analytics • Audit Chain • Kill Switch] subgraph S1[Who is behind the agent?] A end subgraph S2[What is the agent allowed to be?] B end subgraph S3[Can the runtime be trusted?] C end subgraph S4[Can the action happen now?] D end subgraph S5[Can the bank prove and stop it?] E end classDef teal fill:#dff4f2,stroke:#01696f,color:#163235,stroke-width:2px; classDef beige fill:#f3f0ec,stroke:#b9b4ac,color:#28251d,stroke-width:1.5px; class A,B,C,D,E teal; class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 beige;
flowchart TB subgraph Channels[Entry Channels] C1[Customer Agent App] C2[Employee Copilot] C3[Partner / Vendor Agent] C4[Batch / Event Agent] end subgraph Control[Agent Identity and Trust Plane] R1[Agent Registration API] R2[Agent Registry] R3[Principal Link Service] R4[Credential Authority\nSPIFFE / X.509 / JWT-SVID] R5[Attestation Verifier\nimage hash, SBOM, model fingerprint] R6[Delegation Ledger] end subgraph Policy[Policy and Decision Plane] P1[Policy Decision Point] P2[Policy Information Point] P3[Risk Scoring Engine] P4[Capability Catalog] P5[Human Approval Orchestrator] end subgraph Runtime[Execution Plane] E1[Agent Gateway] E2[Tool / API Broker] E3[Workflow Orchestrator] E4[Core Banking Adapter] E5[Payments / Wire Adapter] E6[KYC / KYB / AML Systems] end subgraph Evidence[Monitoring and Evidence Plane] M1[Action Event Bus] M2[Behavior Analytics] M3[Immutable Audit Store] M4[Drift / Abuse Detection] M5[Kill Switch Service] end subgraph Govern[Governance Plane] G1[Compliance Workbench] G2[Ops Console] G3[Model Governance] G4[Vendor Risk Platform] G5[Regulator Evidence Export] end Channels --> R1 R1 --> R2 R1 --> R3 R1 --> R5 R5 --> R4 R3 --> R6 R2 --> P2 R6 --> P2 R4 --> E1 C1 --> E1 C2 --> E1 C3 --> E1 C4 --> E1 E1 --> P1 P2 --> P1 P3 --> P1 P4 --> P1 P5 --> P1 P1 --> E2 E2 --> E3 E3 --> E4 E3 --> E5 E3 --> E6 E1 --> M1 E2 --> M1 E3 --> M1 M1 --> M2 M1 --> M3 M2 --> M4 M4 --> M5 M3 --> G1 M3 --> G2 M3 --> G5 G3 --> R5 G4 --> R1
Infographic runtime

Low-level control conveyor

This view compresses the runtime path into the control checkpoints that matter most for engineering reviews and audit explainability.

flowchart LR U[Agent Workload] --> T[Runtime Trust Check\nimage digest • namespace • model fingerprint] T --> I[Credential Issuance\nX.509-SVID or JWT-SVID] I --> G[Agent Gateway\nmTLS verify • context enrich] G --> P[Policy Decision\nidentity • delegation • risk • capability] P -->|allow| X[Tool and API Broker] P -->|conditional| H[Approval Step] H --> X X --> Y[Target Systems\nKYC • AML • Payments • Core] X --> Z[Action Events] Z --> M[Behavior Analytics] Z --> A[Immutable Audit] M --> K[Kill Switch and Quarantine] classDef trust fill:#dff4f2,stroke:#01696f,color:#163235,stroke-width:2px; classDef decision fill:#eef2fb,stroke:#5d78b8,color:#20304d,stroke-width:2px; classDef execution fill:#f8f3ea,stroke:#b87a28,color:#4a2f12,stroke-width:2px; classDef evidence fill:#f6e8f1,stroke:#a12c7b,color:#4f183b,stroke-width:2px; class U,T,I,G trust; class P,H decision; class X,Y execution; class Z,M,A,K evidence;
Infographic resilience

Emergency containment path

When behavior drift or policy bypass is detected, KYA must revoke trust and halt execution fast enough to reduce financial and control exposure.

flowchart TB A[Behavior Drift\nor Policy Bypass] --> B[Risk Threshold Breached] B --> C[Emergency Response] C --> C1[Revoke Credentials] C --> C2[Deny New Policy Decisions] C --> C3[Quarantine Active Tasks] C --> C4[Open Incident and Notify SOC] C1 --> D[Agent Contained] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D classDef red fill:#fdecef,stroke:#c03d68,color:#5b1830,stroke-width:2px; class A,B,C,C1,C2,C3,C4,D red;

2.1 Logical components

Component Responsibility Implementation notes
Agent Registration API Onboard agent metadata, owner, principal linkage, risk tier, deployment environment, model inventory, tool intents. Exposes bank-internal REST/gRPC APIs, event-driven updates, and workflow hooks to KYC/KYB onboarding journeys.
Agent Registry System of record for agent identities, lifecycle status, assurance level, credential status, policy bindings. Backed by a strongly consistent relational store plus immutable history stream.
Principal Link Service Binds the agent to an already-verified person, business, employee, service domain, or vendor record. Consumes CIF, CRM, HR, vendor master, KYB, and legal entity hierarchies.
Credential Authority Issues short-lived runtime credentials to workloads after attestation and registration checks. SPIFFE/SPIRE-compatible issuance with X.509-SVID and JWT-SVID patterns for mTLS and API auth.
Attestation Verifier Checks code provenance, container image digest, model fingerprint, prompt-pack version, and environment claims. Integrates with CI/CD attestations, artifact signing, and trusted execution evidence where available.
Agent Gateway Single ingress for agent runtime calls, challenge-response identity validation, token exchange, and request enrichment. Injects agent ID, principal ID, delegation chain, session risk, and correlation IDs into every request.
Policy Decision Point Evaluates agent action requests against identity, capability, principal, risk, transaction, and contextual policies. Prefer decoupled policy engine such as OPA/Cedar-style evaluation with decision logs.
Behavior Analytics Scores agent behavior drift, abnormal tool sequences, unusual counterparty patterns, and control bypass attempts. Streams events from all agent interactions and joins them with sanctions, fraud, and case data.
Kill Switch Service Immediately blocks an agent, revokes credentials, quarantines tasks, and freezes active capability grants. Must support sub-minute propagation and emergency operations outside standard change windows.

2.2 Deployment topology

flowchart LR subgraph ZoneA[Customer / Partner DMZ] A1[API Gateway / WAF] A2[Agent Gateway Edge] end subgraph ZoneB[Trust Services VPC / Segment] B1[Registration Service] B2[Registry DB Primary] B3[Credential Authority] B4[Attestation Service] B5[Policy Engine] B6[Delegation Ledger] end subgraph ZoneC[Runtime VPC / OCP / K8s] C1[Agent Workloads] C2[Workflow Engine] C3[Tool Broker] C4[Service Mesh mTLS] end subgraph ZoneD[Enterprise Systems] D1[KYC/KYB] D2[AML / OFAC] D3[Core Banking] D4[Payments] D5[Case Management] end subgraph ZoneE[Monitoring / Forensics] E1[Kafka / Event Bus] E2[SIEM / UEBA] E3[Immutable Audit Store] E4[Evidence Lakehouse] end A1 --> A2 --> B5 A2 --> C1 C1 --> C4 --> C3 C3 --> D1 C3 --> D2 C3 --> D3 C3 --> D4 C3 --> D5 C1 --> B3 C1 --> B4 B1 --> B2 B1 --> B6 B3 --> C4 C1 --> E1 C3 --> E1 B5 --> E1 E1 --> E2 E1 --> E3 E1 --> E4
Reference render

Reference architecture board

Board-style reference render combining logical, external integration, component, and deployment views for stakeholder walkthroughs.

2A. Architecture board

This board turns the KYA design into four explicit architecture views: logical control flow, enterprise integrations, component decomposition, and hybrid deployment topology. The goal is to let architecture review boards, security teams, and platform engineers inspect the same system from different levels of abstraction without losing the trust model.

Board-ready architecture

Multi-view KYA platform

A single board that separates what the platform does, what it connects to, how it is built, and where it runs.

Trust and identity Policy and governance Execution and integration Monitoring and evidence
Panel A

Logical architecture

From onboarding and verification through registry, monitoring, and compliance reporting.

flowchart LR U[Users and Actors] --> O[Onboarding and Enrollment] O --> V[Verification Engine] V --> R[Registry and Identity] R --> M[Monitoring and Analytics] M --> C[Compliance and Reporting] R --> S[Shared Platform Services] S --> D[Data Layer] classDef t fill:#dff4f2,stroke:#01696f,color:#163235,stroke-width:2px; classDef p fill:#eef2fb,stroke:#5d78b8,color:#20304d,stroke-width:2px; classDef e fill:#f8f3ea,stroke:#b87a28,color:#4a2f12,stroke-width:2px; classDef x fill:#f6e8f1,stroke:#a12c7b,color:#4f183b,stroke-width:2px; class U,O,V,R t; class S,D p; class M e; class C x;
Panel B

External integrations

KYA sits between enterprise systems, external vendors, and security operations while keeping the platform as the policy choke point.

Component architecture

Channels, gateway, KYA microservices, event bus, platform services, and state stores are shown as separate concerns.

Deployment topology

The platform spans on-prem systems, a hybrid cloud control plane, security shared services, and DR replication boundaries.

flowchart LR OP[On-prem Core and Legacy] --> HY[Hybrid Cloud KYA Control Plane] HY --> SS[Security and Shared Services] HY --> DR[Cross-region DR] HY --> EC[External Cloud Services] HY --> EU[Edge and End Users] classDef t fill:#dff4f2,stroke:#01696f,color:#163235,stroke-width:2px; classDef p fill:#eef2fb,stroke:#5d78b8,color:#20304d,stroke-width:2px; classDef e fill:#f8f3ea,stroke:#b87a28,color:#4a2f12,stroke-width:2px; class HY t; class SS,DR p; class OP,EC,EU e;
Reference render

Reference trust-chain architecture

High-assurance view of delegated authority, agent identity, gateway enforcement, hardware-backed policy execution, and protected egress.

2B. Trust chain and high-assurance execution

This view formalizes the KYA trust chain from verified principal through delegated authority, agent identity material, gateway enforcement, hardware-backed verification, and protected banking-core execution. It is the right level of detail for zero-trust, IAM, and platform-security stakeholders.

flowchart TB subgraph IDL[Identity Layer] P[Human or Business Principal KYC/KYB Verified] W1[Principal Identity Wallet] VC[Delegation Credential VC or signed mandate] P --> VC --> W1 end subgraph AEL[Agent Execution Layer] AG[Autonomous Agent Instance] W2[Agent Identity Wallet DID and keys] IT[Signed Intent Payload] W1 --> AG W2 --> AG --> IT end subgraph GWL[Enterprise Gateway Layer] GW[KYA Gateway DMZ and middleware] REG[Trust and DID Registry] IT --> GW GW <--> REG end subgraph HAL[Hardware Attested Verification] EN[AWS Nitro Enclave Policy engine and verifier] KMS[KMS / HSM / trust anchors] GW --> EN KMS --> EN end subgraph EGL[Protected Egress] CB[Core Banking and Ledger] PM[Payments and Settlement Rails] EN --> CB EN --> PM end classDef trust fill:#dff4f2,stroke:#01696f,color:#163235,stroke-width:2px; classDef policy fill:#eef2fb,stroke:#5d78b8,color:#20304d,stroke-width:2px; classDef exec fill:#f8f3ea,stroke:#b87a28,color:#4a2f12,stroke-width:2px; classDef ev fill:#f6e8f1,stroke:#a12c7b,color:#4f183b,stroke-width:2px; class P,W1,VC,W2 trust; class AG,IT,GW,REG exec; class EN,KMS policy; class CB,PM ev;
Delegation provenance

The agent does not act on implicit trust; it acts through a signed delegation or enterprise mandate tied back to a verified principal.

Hardware-backed policy

High-assurance execution paths can terminate inside an enclave or equivalent TEE before any validated intent reaches protected settlement systems.

Protected egress

KYA remains the last explainable decision boundary before ledger mutation, payment instruction, or sanctions-sensitive action.

Reference render

Reference token exchange sequence

Protocol render of access-token issuance, proof-of-possession verification, and validated intent routing.

2C. Token exchange and execution protocol

This sequence view defines a protocol-grade handshake for agent access, proof-of-possession, registry lookup, enclave validation, token issuance, and validated execution. It makes the runtime trust model auditable step by step instead of treating token exchange as a black box.

sequenceDiagram participant AG as Autonomous Agent participant GW as KYA Gateway (DMZ) participant DR as DID/VC Registry participant EN as Policy Enclave participant CS as Core Settlement AG->>GW: 1. Request access token (Signed JWT + PKCE challenge) GW->>DR: 2. Resolve DID / delegated identity DR-->>GW: 3. Return DID document, keys, and status GW->>EN: 4. Verify payload, claims, and constraints EN-->>GW: 5. Validation success and enclave proof GW-->>AG: 6. Issue short-lived bound token AG->>GW: 7. Submit transaction intent, token, and DPoP proof GW->>EN: 8. Verify proof-of-possession and scope EN-->>GW: 9. Policy allow and validated intent hash GW->>CS: 10. Route validated intent to protected core CS-->>GW: 11. Accepted reference or result code GW-->>AG: 12. Execution status and evidence reference
Reference render

Reference deterministic validation pipeline

Presentation render of the fail-closed pass/fail validator chain used before protected execution.

2D. Deterministic validation pipeline

This validator turns KYA into a deterministic, fail-closed control system. Any failure in identity, signature, delegation, revocation, capability, scope, or tier checks terminates the request and writes a unified evidence record before protected execution can proceed.

flowchart LR S1[1 Parse Payload] -->|PASS| S2[2 Header Verification] S2 -->|PASS| S3[3 Expiration Preflight] S3 -->|PASS| S4[4 DID Resolution] S4 -->|PASS| S5[5 Verify Signature] S5 -->|PASS| S6[6 Validate Claims] S6 -->|PASS| S7[7 TTL Validation] S7 -->|PASS| S8[8 Revocation Status] S8 -->|PASS| S9[9 Delegation Tree] S9 -->|PASS| S10[10 Capability Manifest] S10 -->|PASS| S11[11 Scope Check] S11 -->|PASS| S12[12 Inheritance and Tier Limits] S12 -->|PASS| OK[Route Validated Intent] S1 -->|FAIL| F[Unified Fail-Closed Termination] S2 -->|FAIL| F S3 -->|FAIL| F S4 -->|FAIL| F S5 -->|FAIL| F S6 -->|FAIL| F S7 -->|FAIL| F S8 -->|FAIL| F S9 -->|FAIL| F S10 -->|FAIL| F S11 -->|FAIL| F S12 -->|FAIL| F F --> RJ[Reject and Log 400 / 401 / 403 + evidence] classDef pass fill:#dff4f2,stroke:#01696f,color:#163235,stroke-width:2px; classDef fail fill:#fdecef,stroke:#c03d68,color:#5b1830,stroke-width:2px; classDef done fill:#f8f3ea,stroke:#b87a28,color:#4a2f12,stroke-width:2px; class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9,S10,S11,S12 pass; class F,RJ fail; class OK done;
Fail closed by default

No downstream adapter should guess or compensate for missing trust signals. Rejection and logging are explicit platform behaviors.

Single evidence path

Success and failure both emit normalized evidence so security operations, audit, and model governance all consume the same canonical event trail.

Scope before execution

Capability, scope, and inheritance checks must happen before any payment, onboarding mutation, case action, or servicing operation is routed to target systems.

3. Canonical data model

The canonical model must support both onboarding-time and runtime enforcement. The key design choice is that the agent is never a free-floating identity. It is always linked to a verified principal, an owning domain, an accountable human or committee, a deployment artifact set, and a capability grant set.

3.1 Entity model

classDiagram class Principal { principal_id principal_type kyc_kyb_ref legal_entity_id status risk_rating } class Agent { agent_id agent_type assurance_level lifecycle_status owner_org_unit accountable_owner_id deployment_env model_profile_id } class Delegation { delegation_id principal_id agent_id intent_scope limits valid_from valid_to revocation_state } class Credential { credential_id agent_id spiffe_id key_type issued_at expires_at cert_thumbprint status } class CapabilityGrant { grant_id agent_id capability_code resource_selector tx_limit approval_mode policy_ref } class ModelProfile { model_profile_id vendor model_name version fingerprint prompt_pack_hash safety_profile } class RuntimeSession { session_id agent_id principal_id credential_id risk_score current_state } class ActionEvent { event_id session_id action_type target_system target_resource decision_id outcome evidence_hash } Principal "1" --> "many" Delegation Agent "1" --> "many" Delegation Agent "1" --> "many" Credential Agent "1" --> "many" CapabilityGrant Agent "many" --> "1" ModelProfile Agent "1" --> "many" RuntimeSession RuntimeSession "1" --> "many" ActionEvent

3.2 Core tables

agent_registry

agent_id PK
agent_name
agent_type            -- customer, employee, partner, internal-system
assurance_level       -- A1..A4
lifecycle_status      -- draft, pending_review, active, suspended, revoked, retired
principal_binding_mode -- direct, delegated, pooled, service-domain
owner_org_unit
accountable_owner_id
deployment_env        -- dev, uat, prod
risk_tier             -- low, medium, high, critical
model_profile_id FK
created_at
updated_at

agent_delegation

delegation_id PK
agent_id FK
principal_id FK
delegation_type       -- explicit_consent, contractual, employment, system
intent_scope_json
jurisdiction_scope
transaction_limits_json
approval_requirements_json
valid_from
valid_to
revoked_at
revocation_reason

agent_credential

credential_id PK
agent_id FK
spiffe_id
credential_format     -- x509_svid, jwt_svid, oauth_dpop
attestation_ref
public_key_thumbprint
issued_at
expires_at
revoked_at
status

agent_action_event

event_id PK
session_id
agent_id
principal_id
action_type
resource_type
resource_id
policy_decision_id
approval_ticket_id
result_code
risk_score
event_ts
evidence_hash
chain_hash

3.3 Example registration payload

{
  "agent_name": "treasury-cash-positioning-agent",
  "agent_type": "internal-system",
  "principal_binding_mode": "service-domain",
  "principal_ref": {
    "principal_type": "legal_entity",
    "kyb_ref": "LE-9821734",
    "business_unit": "Global Treasury"
  },
  "accountable_owner": {
    "employee_id": "E102938",
    "role": "Treasury Product Owner"
  },
  "deployment": {
    "environment": "prod",
    "cluster": "ocp-prod-east-1",
    "namespace": "treasury-agents",
    "service_account": "cash-positioning-agent-sa"
  },
  "model_profile": {
    "vendor": "internal-hosted",
    "model_name": "llm-exec-70b",
    "version": "2026.05",
    "fingerprint": "sha256:ab34...",
    "prompt_pack_hash": "sha256:9ad1..."
  },
  "capability_requests": [
    {
      "capability_code": "READ_INTRADAY_BALANCES",
      "resource_selector": "acct-group:nostro-usd",
      "approval_mode": "auto",
      "transaction_limit_usd": 0
    },
    {
      "capability_code": "CREATE_FUNDING_RECOMMENDATION",
      "resource_selector": "desk:treasury-liquidity",
      "approval_mode": "human_required",
      "transaction_limit_usd": 5000000
    }
  ]
}

4. Core sequence flows

The low-level behavior is driven by explicit gates at registration, credential issuance, tool invocation, and post-action monitoring. Every high-risk action is a composition of identity proof, delegation validation, policy evaluation, and evidence emission.

4.1 Agent onboarding during KYC/KYB journey

sequenceDiagram participant U as Customer / Business User participant O as Onboarding Portal participant K as KYC/KYB Platform participant R as KYA Registration API participant P as Principal Link Service participant C as Compliance Reviewer participant G as Agent Registry participant A as Credential Authority U->>O: Register account / business and declare AI agent usage O->>K: Submit KYC/KYB package K-->>O: Verified principal / pending review O->>R: Submit agent registration draft R->>P: Resolve principal binding P-->>R: principal_id + legal entity context R->>G: Create agent with pending_review status R->>C: Open KYA review task C->>R: Approve risk tier, capabilities, owner, controls R->>G: Activate agent identity R->>A: Enable credential issuance policy A-->>R: issuance profile created R-->>O: KYA completed, agent_id returned

4.2 Runtime credential issuance

sequenceDiagram participant W as Agent Workload participant S as SPIRE / Workload Attestor participant V as Attestation Verifier participant R as Agent Registry participant D as Delegation Service participant CA as Credential Authority W->>S: Present workload attestation evidence S->>V: Verify image digest, namespace, SA, node, SBOM V->>R: Check agent active + env binding V->>D: Check valid delegation + assurance level D-->>V: delegation valid R-->>V: registry valid V-->>S: issuance allowed S->>CA: CSR / token request CA-->>W: short-lived X.509-SVID / JWT-SVID W->>W: store in memory only

4.3 High-risk action with human approval

sequenceDiagram participant Ag as Agent participant Gw as Agent Gateway participant Pd as Policy Decision Point participant Rs as Risk Engine participant Ha as Human Approval Orchestrator participant Op as Ops / Business Approver participant Tb as Tool Broker participant Ps as Payment System participant Au as Audit Store Ag->>Gw: Request initiate_wire_transfer(action, context) Gw->>Pd: Evaluate with agent_id, principal_id, credential, tx amount Pd->>Rs: Get risk score and behavioral context Rs-->>Pd: elevated risk = 82 Pd-->>Gw: decision = CONDITIONAL_APPROVAL Gw->>Ha: Create approval task with evidence bundle Ha->>Op: Present action summary, policy basis, provenance Op-->>Ha: Approve with step-up auth Ha-->>Gw: approval token issued Gw->>Tb: Execute with approval token + delegated scope Tb->>Ps: Invoke payment API Ps-->>Tb: accepted / reference id Tb-->>Gw: success Gw->>Au: Write signed audit record Gw-->>Ag: completion result

4.4 Drift detection and emergency revocation

sequenceDiagram participant Ev as Event Stream participant Ba as Behavior Analytics participant Dr as Drift Detector participant Ks as Kill Switch participant Ca as Credential Authority participant Pe as Policy Engine participant So as SOC / Compliance Ops Ev->>Ba: action events + prompts/tools metadata Ba->>Dr: evaluate sequence anomaly and policy bypass indicators Dr-->>Ks: critical anomaly detected Ks->>Ca: revoke active credentials Ks->>Pe: deny all future decisions for agent_id Ks->>So: page on-call + open incident So-->>Ks: confirm quarantine / investigate

5. Control design

5.1 Assurance levels

Level Use case Identity proof Control expectation
A1 Read-only retrieval, knowledge assistance, low-risk case triage. Registered agent, owner mapped, basic workload identity. No write access, low-risk tools only, standard logging.
A2 Workflow drafting, case preparation, document extraction, KYC enrichment. Attested workload, model profile fixed, explicit capability grants. Writes to non-authoritative staging systems, reviewer sign-off for finalization.
A3 Operational execution in bounded domains such as account servicing or remediation. Short-lived credentials, verified delegation, continuous monitoring. Transaction thresholds, dual control, dynamic risk evaluation, immutable evidence.
A4 Funds movement, sanctions-sensitive actions, privileged treasury or market operations. Strong attestation, step-up approval, per-action intent tokens, highest scrutiny. Mandatory human authorization or dual-agent plus human control, just-in-time scope, instant revocation.

5.2 Policy dimensions

Identity policy

  • Agent lifecycle status.
  • Assurance level minimums.
  • Environment and namespace binding.
  • Credential freshness and revocation state.

Delegation policy

  • Principal consent or institutional mandate.
  • Jurisdiction and legal entity boundaries.
  • Task intent scope.
  • Start/end time and revocation windows.

Runtime policy

  • Tool allow-list and resource selectors.
  • Amount, frequency, and counterparty thresholds.
  • Human approval rules.
  • Behavior anomaly score thresholds.

5.3 Example authorization policy

package kya.authz

default allow = false

action := input.action
agent := input.agent
principal := input.principal
risk := input.risk

auto_allow {
  agent.lifecycle_status == "active"
  agent.assurance_level in ["A2", "A3", "A4"]
  input.credential.revoked == false
  action.capability_code == "READ_KYC_CASE"
  action.resource.legal_entity_id == principal.legal_entity_id
}

require_human_approval {
  action.capability_code == "INITIATE_PAYMENT"
  risk.score >= 60
}

deny_reason[msg] {
  input.delegation.valid == false
  msg := "delegation_invalid"
}

deny_reason[msg] {
  input.model_profile.fingerprint != input.attestation.model_fingerprint
  msg := "model_fingerprint_mismatch"
}

allow {
  auto_allow
  not require_human_approval
}
Critical guardrail: An agent must never inherit a human's standing entitlements wholesale. Delegation must be transformed into a reduced capability set with explicit task, data, channel, amount, and time constraints.

6. Implementation blueprint

Platform implementation

Component-level infographic

This modular view shows how registry, delegation, attestation, credentials, policy, monitoring, and evidence services form the KYA platform core.

Infographic components

KYA platform blueprint

Enterprise sources feed the control plane, which then drives runtime enforcement through the gateway and broker layers.

flowchart TB subgraph CORE[KYA Platform Components] R[Registry Service\n\nagents • lifecycle • owner • risk tier] D[Delegation Service\n\nprincipal linkage • consent • limits] A[Attestation Service\n\nartifact verify • model verify • env verify] C[Credential Broker\n\nissue • exchange • revoke] P[Policy Service\n\ndecide • simulate • explain] M[Monitoring Service\n\nanomaly • drift • quarantine] E[Evidence Service\n\naudit chain • export • search] end subgraph SOURCES[Enterprise Sources] S1[KYC and KYB] S2[HR • Vendor • CIF] S3[Model Governance] S4[Risk Signals] end subgraph RUNTIME[Runtime Enforcement] G[Agent Gateway] B[Tool Broker] T[Protected Systems] end S1 --> R S2 --> D S3 --> A S4 --> P R --> P D --> P A --> C C --> G P --> G G --> B B --> T G --> M B --> E M --> E classDef core fill:#dff4f2,stroke:#01696f,color:#163235,stroke-width:2px; classDef src fill:#f3f0ec,stroke:#b9b4ac,color:#28251d,stroke-width:1.5px; classDef run fill:#eef2fb,stroke:#5d78b8,color:#20304d,stroke-width:2px; class R,D,A,C,P,M,E core; class S1,S2,S3,S4 src; class G,B,T run;

6.1 Service decomposition

Service API surface State Recommended stack
kya-registry-service /agents, /delegations, /capabilities, /lifecycle PostgreSQL + CDC stream Java / Go, relational DB, outbox pattern
kya-attestation-service /verify/workload, /verify/model, /verify/artifact Artifact metadata cache Go / Rust, Sigstore integrations, SBOM verification
kya-credential-broker /issue, /exchange, /revoke Minimal; trust anchors only SPIRE-based issuance, HSM-backed CA keys
kya-policy-service /decide, /simulate, /explain Policy bundles + decision logs OPA / Cedar evaluator, Redis cache for policy info
kya-monitoring-service /score, /alerts, /quarantine Feature store + stream state Kafka/Flink/Spark streaming, anomaly detection models
kya-evidence-service /export, /chain, /search Object store + append-only index S3/WORM, OpenSearch, signed manifests

6.2 Runtime security pattern

  1. Agent workload starts on an attested cluster or VM node with service mesh sidecar.
  2. Workload attestor maps namespace + service account + image digest to a pre-registered agent identity.
  3. Credential broker issues a short-lived X.509-SVID or JWT-SVID.
  4. Agent Gateway terminates inbound traffic, verifies mTLS, enriches with agent and principal claims, and requests a policy decision.
  5. Tool Broker performs secondary authorization at the target tool boundary and emits action events.
  6. Any delegated write or transaction action receives a one-time intent token bound to action hash and expiration.

6.3 Minimal API contract

POST /v1/agents
POST /v1/agents/{agentId}/activate
POST /v1/agents/{agentId}/suspend
POST /v1/delegations
POST /v1/delegations/{delegationId}/revoke
POST /v1/policy/decide
POST /v1/credentials/exchange
POST /v1/actions/approve
POST /v1/actions/{actionId}/quarantine
GET  /v1/evidence/{eventId}
GET  /v1/agents/{agentId}/audit-trail

6.4 Decision request envelope

{
  "request_id": "8bb6f4c7-6e09-4c4a-b53d-7c721caa0181",
  "agent": {
    "agent_id": "agt_01JXZ...",
    "assurance_level": "A3",
    "lifecycle_status": "active",
    "risk_tier": "high"
  },
  "principal": {
    "principal_id": "bus_20491",
    "principal_type": "legal_entity",
    "legal_entity_id": "LE-9821734"
  },
  "credential": {
    "spiffe_id": "spiffe://bank.example/ns/treasury/sa/cash-positioning-agent",
    "issued_at": "2026-06-14T08:15:10Z",
    "expires_at": "2026-06-14T09:15:10Z"
  },
  "attestation": {
    "image_digest": "sha256:...",
    "model_fingerprint": "sha256:ab34...",
    "prompt_pack_hash": "sha256:9ad1..."
  },
  "action": {
    "capability_code": "INITIATE_PAYMENT",
    "amount": 250000,
    "currency": "USD",
    "counterparty_id": "CP-9291",
    "channel": "wire"
  },
  "context": {
    "session_risk": 72,
    "geo": "US",
    "time_window": "business-hours",
    "change_ticket": "CHG-773219"
  }
}

7. Operations, observability, and resiliency

7.1 SLOs and reliability targets

Capability Target Comment
Policy decision p95 latency < 50 ms Cached registry and delegation context required.
Credential issuance p95 < 300 ms Attestation verification path must be optimized and pre-warmed.
Kill switch propagation < 60 seconds Revocation should push to gateway, broker, mesh, and policy caches.
Audit event durability Zero silent loss Use append-only log with retry semantics and dead-letter handling.
Evidence export integrity Cryptographically verifiable Every export bundle signed and chain-validated.

7.2 Monitoring dimensions

Technical telemetry

  • Credential issuance rate and failure causes.
  • Registry lifecycle transitions.
  • Policy decision allow/deny/conditional mix.
  • Agent-tool call graph and latency.
  • Revocation lag and stale credential attempts.

Risk telemetry

  • Delegation expiration violations.
  • Model fingerprint mismatches.
  • Prompt-pack drift versus approved release.
  • Unusual counterparty or amount patterns.
  • Repeated approval escalation requests by agent or owner.

7.3 Failure modes

Failure mode Impact Fallback
Policy service unavailable Risk of blind execution Fail closed for write/transaction actions, optionally fail open for selected read-only A1 actions with cached policy and emergency waiver.
Credential authority degraded Agents cannot refresh identity Allow grace window only for low-risk sessions; freeze new high-risk sessions.
Registry replication lag Stale lifecycle / delegation state Decision engine requires freshness threshold; otherwise conditional deny.
Audit sink outage Evidence gap Local append-only buffer plus backpressure on high-risk actions.
Behavior model false positives Operational noise Tiered quarantine, explainability metadata, SOC tuning playbooks.

8. Regulatory and governance alignment

The institution should treat KYA as a composite control aligned to BSA/AML, sanctions controls, cybersecurity requirements, model governance, third-party risk, and enterprise auditability. The regulatory posture improves when the bank can prove who the agent was, which principal it represented, which capabilities it held, how it was technically attested, which decision policy allowed the action, and how the action was monitored afterward.

Control area KYA contribution Evidence artifacts
KYC / KYB / CIP / CDD Links autonomous agent action to an already verified person or business and enforces no-orphan-agent rule. Principal binding record, consent/delegation artifact, onboarding approvals.
AML / OFAC Prevents unverified or over-entitled agents from initiating case or transaction actions and supports explainable adjudication. Decision logs, action provenance, approval records, sanctions check trace.
Cybersecurity / zero trust Uses cryptographic workload identity, short-lived credentials, mTLS, scoped authorization, and revocation. Certificate issuance logs, attestation reports, revocation events.
Model / AI governance Ties runtime actions to approved model profiles, prompt packs, safety profiles, and monitoring thresholds. Model profile registry, validation report links, drift alerts.
Third-party risk Registers partner and vendor agents separately with contractually bounded capabilities. Vendor assessment, assurance level, external attestation, SLA mapping.
Internal audit / exam management Creates forensically reliable, chain-linked evidence packages for examiners and auditors. Immutable audit export bundle, signed manifests, chain verification report.
Governance recommendation: create a cross-functional KYA control owner group spanning IAM, AML compliance, cybersecurity, enterprise architecture, model risk, legal, third-party risk, and platform engineering. No single function has the complete risk picture on its own.

9. Rollout plan

9.1 Phased delivery

Phase Duration Outcomes
Phase 0: Inventory and policy baseline 6-8 weeks Discover all existing AI agents, map owners, classify use cases, define assurance model, choose trust anchors.
Phase 1: Registration and read-only control plane 8-12 weeks Registry, principal binding, lifecycle workflows, evidence foundation, low-risk A1/A2 use cases.
Phase 2: Runtime identity and policy enforcement 12-16 weeks Credential broker, attestation, gateway, PDP integration, tool broker for selected systems.
Phase 3: High-risk workflow controls 12-20 weeks Human approvals, amount thresholds, anomaly detection, kill switch, audit export for exam support.
Phase 4: Full enterprise adoption Ongoing Partner agents, customer-declared agents, federated trust, policy simulation, continuous control testing.

9.2 Adoption strategy

  • Start with internal read/write non-monetary agents where ownership is clear and blast radius is bounded.
  • Insert KYA checks at onboarding portals and runtime gateways before integrating into every downstream legacy system.
  • Define a mandatory enterprise registration standard for any agent with API credentials, workflow triggers, or customer interaction.
  • Require vendor contracts to disclose agentic components, runtime identity methods, and emergency revocation support.
  • Measure control effectiveness with quarterly evidence drills and simulated rogue-agent exercises.

10. Source-informed assumptions

This design uses external industry and standards signals for the core KYA framing, including the idea that KYA must establish agent identity, principal linkage, scoped capability, and accountable ownership; the need for lifecycle governance and interaction governance for financial-service agents; and the use of cryptographic workload identity, short-lived credentials, SPIFFE-compatible identities, attestation, and tamper-evident logs for regulated environments.
